pattern: Turn 'sum of squares of every subarray's distinct count' into a running contribution problem: track how each new element shifts the distinct-count of every valid subarray start, using a range-update/range-sum Fenwick tree.
hints: Squaring a running distinct-count for every one of the O(n^2) subarrays directly is too slow at this size — look for how the distinct count changes only for a contiguous range of subarray starts each time you extend the right end by one element.

When you append a new value, only the starts strictly after its most recent previous occurrence see their distinct count go up by one; use the identity (x+1)^2 = x^2 + 2x + 1 to update a running sum of squares in O(1) once you know the sum over that affected range.

Maintain the per-start distinct counts as a Fenwick tree that supports 'add 1 over a range' and 'sum over a range'; each time you extend the right end, query the sum over the affected range before updating it, use that to update a running total sum-of-squares, then add that running total to the answer once per right endpoint.

edge_inputs.description: a repeated value reappears after other distinct values, exercising the 'range since last occurrence' update
a palindrome-like pattern so several values repeat at different offsets

stress.description: large arrays drawn from varying alphabet sizes to exercise both very sparse and very dense duplicate patterns
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    max_val = rng.choice([5, 50, 1000, 100000])
    nums = [rng.randint(1, max_val) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
